Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Problem: Mobile GPUs use tile-based rendering architectures but existing shading strategies apply uniform shading rates across all tiles, wasting compute on low-complexity regions and undersampling high-detail areas.
Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Existing Methods: Variable Rate Shading (VRS) on desktop GPUs allows per-tile shading rate control, but mobile implementations are limited. Existing mobile approaches include simple screen-space heuristics for shading rate selection and fixed-function MSAA.
Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Motivation: Mobile GPUs have strict power and bandwidth budgets. By analyzing per-tile content complexity (edge density, material variation, motion vectors) at minimal cost, we can dynamically assign shading rates that concentrate compute on visually important regions while saving power on uniform areas.
Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Proposed Method: We propose a lightweight tile complexity analysis pass that runs before the main shading pass. For each tile, we compute a complexity score based on depth discontinuities, normal variation, and temporal motion vectors from the previous frame. Tiles with low complexity use coarse shading (1 shade per 2x2 or 4x4 pixels), while high-complexity tiles use full-rate shading. The complexity analysis uses a small compute shader that operates on the G-buffer from the previous frame, adding minimal overhead. We also introduce a temporal stability mechanism that smoothly transitions shading rates across frames to avoid flickering.
Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Experiment Plan: Test on mobile devices (Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 with Adreno GPU, MediaTek Dimensity 9200 with Mali GPU) using standard test scenes (Sponza, Bistro, San Miguel). Compare against uniform full-rate shading and existing VRS implementations. Measure FPS, power consumption, and visual quality (PSNR, SSIM against full-rate reference).

Neural Texture Compression for Mobile.Problem: Mobile devices have limited GPU memory and bandwidth, making high-quality texture storage and streaming a major bottleneck for visually rich 3D applications.
Neural Texture Compression for Mobile.Existing Methods: Standard texture compression formats (ASTC, ETC2) provide fixed compression ratios with acceptable quality. Neural texture compression methods like NTC achieve better ratios but require expensive decompression that is infeasible on mobile GPUs in real time.
Neural Texture Compression for Mobile.Motivation: We can design a neural texture representation that is specifically optimized for mobile GPU architectures. By using tiny neural networks that fit within the GPU's shader core capabilities and leveraging the tile-based memory hierarchy of mobile GPUs, we can achieve higher compression ratios than ASTC while maintaining real-time decompression.
Neural Texture Compression for Mobile.Proposed Method: We propose MobileNTC, a neural texture compression scheme where each texture is encoded as a compact latent grid plus a tiny decoder network (< 50K parameters). The decoder is designed as a series of 1x1 convolutions that map directly to efficient shader operations on mobile GPUs. During rendering, texture lookups sample from the latent grid and run the decoder in the fragment shader. We train the encoder-decoder per-texture using a combination of L1 loss and perceptual loss (LPIPS). We also introduce a hardware-aware quantization scheme that quantizes decoder weights to formats natively supported by mobile GPU ALUs (FP16, INT8).
Neural Texture Compression for Mobile.Experiment Plan: Evaluate on standard texture datasets and in-game texture atlases. Compare against ASTC at various quality levels and desktop NTC methods. Measure compression ratio, decompression speed (ms per texture fetch), visual quality (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS), and GPU memory savings on Qualcomm Adreno and ARM Mali GPUs.

Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Problem: Mobile games must balance visual quality, frame rate, and battery life. Current approaches either target a fixed frame rate or use simple throttling when the device overheats, leading to inconsistent user experience.
Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Existing Methods: Dynamic resolution scaling (DRS) adjusts render resolution to hit frame targets. Some engines use simple heuristics to reduce quality when battery is low. Academic work on DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling) controls GPU clock speed but doesn't consider rendering workload characteristics.
Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Motivation: By jointly modeling the relationship between rendering parameters (resolution, LOD, shader complexity, post-processing) and their impact on both frame time and power consumption, we can make intelligent quality-performance tradeoffs that maximize visual quality under a user-specified power budget.
Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Proposed Method: We build a lightweight runtime model that predicts frame time and power consumption for different rendering configurations. The model is a small decision tree trained offline on profiling data from the target device. At runtime, before each frame, we query the model with the current scene characteristics (draw call count, triangle count, overdraw estimate) and select the rendering configuration that maximizes a quality objective function subject to the power budget constraint. The quality function weighs resolution, LOD distance, shadow quality, and post-processing effects based on their perceptual impact. We use a feedback loop from actual frame times and battery drain to adapt the model online.
Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Experiment Plan: Implement in a custom mobile renderer on Android. Test on 3-4 mobile devices with different GPU architectures. Evaluate against fixed-quality and DRS-only baselines. Measure sustained FPS over 30-minute sessions, total power consumption, visual quality stability, and user-perceived smoothness.

Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Problem: Occlusion culling on mobile platforms is challenging because CPU-based methods add latency and GPU-based occlusion queries are expensive on tile-based mobile GPUs due to the deferred nature of rendering.
Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Existing Methods: Software occlusion culling rasterizes a depth buffer on the CPU. GPU-based methods use conditional rendering or occlusion queries but suffer from GPU-CPU round-trip latency. Hierarchical Z-buffer (Hi-Z) approaches work well on desktop but are less efficient on mobile tile-based architectures.
Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Motivation: We can exploit the tile-based architecture of mobile GPUs by building a lightweight hierarchical depth representation from the previous frame's depth buffer, then using it to cull objects before they enter the GPU pipeline. By keeping the culling entirely on the GPU using compute shaders, we avoid CPU-GPU synchronization overhead.
Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Proposed Method: We propose a two-phase GPU-driven culling approach. Phase 1: After each frame, a compute shader builds a hierarchical depth pyramid (Hi-Z) from the depth buffer. Phase 2: Before the next frame, another compute shader tests each object's bounding box against the Hi-Z pyramid and writes visible object indices to an indirect draw buffer. The GPU then executes only the visible draw calls via indirect rendering. To handle temporal coherence, objects that were visible in the previous frame are rendered first (as conservative occluders), then newly visible objects are added in a second pass. We optimize the Hi-Z construction for mobile by using the tile memory of the GPU to reduce bandwidth.
Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Experiment Plan: Test on complex game scenes (10K-100K objects) on Adreno, Mali, and Apple GPUs. Compare against no culling, CPU frustum culling, and GPU occlusion queries. Measure draw call reduction ratio, frame time improvement, and power savings.

Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Problem: Mobile games often render at reduced resolution to meet frame rate targets, resulting in blurry or aliased output. Existing temporal upsampling methods like DLSS and FSR are designed for desktop GPUs and are too expensive for mobile.
Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Existing Methods: AMD FSR 1.0 provides spatial upsampling but lacks temporal stability. NVIDIA DLSS uses a heavyweight neural network requiring tensor cores. Mobile engines typically use simple bilinear or bicubic upsampling with optional FXAA.
Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Motivation: By designing a temporal upsampling algorithm specifically for mobile GPU architectures, we can achieve high-quality upsampling with minimal compute overhead. The key insight is to leverage jittered rendering (already common in TAA) combined with a lightweight recurrent filter that accumulates sub-pixel detail across frames.
Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Proposed Method: We propose MobileSR, a temporal super-resolution method for mobile. Each frame is rendered at a lower internal resolution with per-frame sub-pixel jitter. A lightweight compute shader accumulates samples into a history buffer at the output resolution, using motion vectors for reprojection. The accumulation uses a confidence-weighted blend that detects and handles disoccluded regions and fast motion. For areas with no valid history, we fall back to a simple edge-directed spatial upsampler. The entire pipeline adds only 2 compute passes (reprojection + accumulation) and uses ~1MB of additional memory for the history buffer. We specifically optimize memory access patterns for tile-based mobile GPUs.
Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Experiment Plan: Test 2x and 4x upsampling on standard game scenes rendered on mobile devices. Compare against bilinear upsampling, FSR 1.0, and TAA-based approaches. Measure PSNR/SSIM against native resolution reference, frame time overhead, temporal stability (flicker metric), and power consumption on Snapdragon and Apple A-series chips.

Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Problem: Real-time global illumination (GI) on mobile devices is extremely challenging due to the high computational cost of light transport simulation, yet the absence of GI makes mobile game scenes look flat and unrealistic.
Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Existing Methods: Baked lightmaps provide static GI but cannot handle dynamic objects or lighting changes. Screen-space methods (SSAO, SSGI) are limited in range and accuracy. Probe-based methods like Lumen and DDGI require significant compute resources designed for current-gen consoles and PCs.
Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Motivation: We can design a highly simplified probe-based GI system that trades off some accuracy for dramatic performance gains on mobile. By using a sparse probe grid, low-resolution probe updates, and temporal amortization (updating only a few probes per frame), we can provide convincing diffuse GI at mobile-friendly costs.
Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Proposed Method: We place irradiance probes on a sparse world-space grid. Each probe stores a low-resolution (4x4) octahedral irradiance map and a depth map for visibility testing. Instead of ray tracing, we render tiny cubemap snapshots (16x16 per face) for probe updates using simplified scene geometry (LOD0 proxies). Only 2-4 probes are updated per frame via temporal round-robin, amortizing cost across frames. At shading time, we interpolate irradiance from the 8 nearest probes using trilinear weights modified by visibility. The entire system is implemented using compute shaders and adds roughly 0.5ms per frame on mid-range mobile GPUs.
Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Experiment Plan: Test on indoor and outdoor game scenes with dynamic lighting on mobile devices. Compare against no GI, baked lightmaps, and SSAO-only approaches. Measure visual quality (compare against path-traced reference using PSNR, SSIM), frame time overhead, and power consumption. Also evaluate temporal stability of the GI signal.

Mesh Shader Emulation on Mobile via Comp.Problem: Mesh shaders enable GPU-driven geometry processing and fine-grained culling on modern desktop GPUs, but mobile GPUs do not support mesh shaders, preventing mobile applications from benefiting from these optimizations.
Mesh Shader Emulation on Mobile via Comp.Existing Methods: Traditional mobile rendering uses vertex shaders with index buffers. Some mobile engines implement GPU-driven rendering via compute-based culling followed by indirect draws, but without the flexible geometry processing that mesh shaders provide.
Mesh Shader Emulation on Mobile via Comp.Motivation: We can emulate the core functionality of mesh shaders using compute shaders that are widely available on mobile GPUs (via Vulkan or Metal). By processing meshlets in compute shaders and outputting to intermediate buffers, we can achieve similar benefits: fine-grained per-meshlet culling, LOD selection, and geometry amplification/simplification.
Mesh Shader Emulation on Mobile via Comp.Proposed Method: We decompose meshes into meshlets (groups of ~64 triangles) at asset build time. At runtime, a compute shader processes each meshlet: performing frustum and backface culling, selecting LOD level, and optionally simplifying geometry. Surviving meshlets write their triangles to a global index buffer, which is then rendered with a single indirect draw call. For mobile-specific optimization, we pack meshlet data to align with GPU cache lines and use shared memory to reduce global memory traffic. We also propose a two-level culling strategy: coarse per-cluster culling followed by fine per-meshlet culling.
Mesh Shader Emulation on Mobile via Comp.Experiment Plan: Test on game-quality meshes ranging from 100K to 10M triangles on Vulkan-capable Android devices and Metal-capable iOS devices. Compare against traditional vertex pipeline with CPU frustum culling. Measure triangle throughput, draw call reduction, frame time, and memory bandwidth usage.

Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Problem: Shadow mapping is one of the most bandwidth-intensive operations in mobile rendering, as it requires additional depth-only rendering passes and shadow map sampling during shading, both of which stress the limited memory bandwidth of mobile GPUs.
Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Existing Methods: Cascaded Shadow Maps (CSM) are standard but multiply the bandwidth cost. Variance Shadow Maps (VSM) allow filtering but require floating-point textures. Screen-space shadow techniques reduce bandwidth but produce artifacts.
Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Motivation: Mobile GPUs have tile-based architectures where on-chip tile memory is fast but off-chip bandwidth is expensive and power-hungry. By redesigning shadow mapping to minimize off-chip memory traffic—keeping shadow computations within the tile where possible—we can achieve high-quality shadows at a fraction of the bandwidth cost.
Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Proposed Method: We propose Tiled Shadow Mapping (TSM), which computes shadows per-tile rather than per-light. For each screen tile, we identify which shadow cascades are needed, and only fetch the relevant shadow map texels. We store a compact per-tile shadow mask (1 bit per sample) in tile memory, which is then used during the shading pass without additional shadow map fetches. For the shadow map generation pass, we use a single atlas with tight per-light viewport packing to minimize wasted bandwidth. We also introduce a temporal shadow caching scheme where static shadow regions are cached and only dynamic shadow casters trigger re-rendering.
Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Experiment Plan: Test on outdoor game scenes with directional and point lights on mobile devices. Compare against standard CSM, VSM, and screen-space shadows. Measure shadow quality (compare against high-res reference), frame time, memory bandwidth (via GPU profiler), and power consumption.
